Role/Function: Earth Mother
Notes: Prithvi Mata in the Vedic tradition is the consort of Dyaus Pita and mother of many gods, including Indra.

Role/Function: Twin horsemen gods associated with fertility, oaths, sons of the sky god, and healing
Notes: Twin sons of Dyḗws, protectors of oaths and divine aid in battle, often depicted as horsemen. Reflected in the Vedic Ashvins and the Greek Dioscuri.

Role/Function: Sky Father, chief god
Notes: Dyēus Pətḗr is the Sky Father, overseeing justice, honor, and order in the Proto-Indo-European pantheon. His sacred animals include the eagle, wolf, lion, bull, and swan. Dyēus governs the day-lit sky, and his symbols include the shield, sky-blue color, and eagle. He is a patron of kings, inspiring leadership and law. He is father to Áusōs and other deities. Often called Dyáuṣ Pitṛ́ in Vedic tradition, associated with the daylight sky, consort of Prithvi (Earth) and father of Indra, Surya, and Ushas. Indra, a thunder and warrior god, is born from Dyaus and Prithvi, analogous to Zeus being born in later Greek theogonies. Indra shares thunderous attributes with Zeus.

Role/Function: Fire god, sacrificial fire
Notes: The Proto-Indo-European god of fire, associated with both terrestrial and celestial flames. In Vedic mythology, Agni is the god of fire, and similar deities include Hephaestus (fire aspect), Loki (in his fire associations), and Svarog.

Role/Function: Primordial god of the sky and cosmic order
Notes: Áḱmōn is the primordial sky god, said to be begotten from the skull of Yemos and tied to the upper realms of the sky. He represents order in the cosmos.

Role/Function: Water god, divine herder
Notes: Apám Nápāt in the Vedas is a fire deity residing in water. God of waters, especially connected to the sea and rivers.

Role/Function: God of hospitality and welfare
Notes: The Proto-Indo-European deity *H₂eryo-men is responsible for the welfare of the community, marital ties, and pathways/roads. In the Vedic tradition, Aryaman is the god of hospitality and marriage, reflecting similar functions. In Iranian traditions, Airyaman represents the tribal network and is invoked for healing and protection against illness and evil. The Irish hero Érimón, who became the first king of the Milesians, also shares aspects of *H₂eryo-men's role, especially concerning marriage and social order.

Role/Function: Dawn goddess
Notes: Daughter of *Dyēus, the goddess of dawn, often depicted as a beautiful young woman bringing light and renewing the day. Various dawn goddesses exist in multiple Indo-European traditions. Ushas in Vedic tradition is the daughter of Dyēus and brings the dawn; Ushas is also described as dancing and wearing red garments.

Role/Function: Hero, Primal man
Notes: Anthropogenic deity, reconstruction uncertain.

Role/Function: God of wind and storm
Notes: *H₂weh₁-yú, derived from the PIE root h₂weh₁- meaning "to blow" or "breathe," is a wind deity. In Indo-Iranian tradition, Vayu-Vāta represents wind as a dual divinity, with Vayu associated with controlled winds and Vāta linked to violent, stormy winds. Other cognates include Aeolus in Greek myth and Venti in Roman tradition. The deity's role varies slightly across cultures but is consistently associated with wind or storm phenomena.

Role/Function: Ancestor of mankind, first man
Notes: Possibly related to *Yemo-, but distinct role unclear.

Role/Function: Moon deity, ruler of the night
Other groups: Albanian: Hënë, Phrygian: Men, Hittite: Kašku
Notes: Personification of the Moon, often in a sibling relationship with the Sun.

Role/Function: Three-headed serpent, enemy of *Trito, cattle thief
Notes: The three-headed serpent who steals the cattle from *Trito. Represents chaos and the cosmic struggle between gods and heroes. Trito defeats *Ngʷhi and returns the cattle to the priest.

Role/Function: Guardian of herds, protector
Notes: Pūshān in the Vedic tradition is the protector of roads and herds, often associated with journeys.

Role/Function: Thunder god
Notes: Often depicted holding a weapon, linked to oak trees and mountains. Indra, born from Dyaus and Prithvi, takes on this role in Vedic tradition, akin to Zeus in Greek myth, ruling storms and thunder.

Role/Function: Sun deity, god(dess) of light and life
Other groups: Etruscan: Usil, Hittite: UTU-liya, Hittite: Ištanu
Notes: Deity of the Sun, associated with the solar disk, light, warmth, and life. Frequently depicted driving a chariot across the sky. Celebrated on Sunday.

Role/Function: Sea god
Notes: A sea deity with thematic echoes in Greek Triton and Roman mythology.

Role/Function: Warrior hero, slayer of serpent, Third brother, dragon-slayer
Notes: A mythic hero who recovers cattle from a serpent; Trita in the Vedic tradition reflects this.

Role/Function: Smith god, craftsman
Notes: The Proto-Indo-European smith god is not fully reconstructed, but examples include Tvastr in Vedic myth, Hephaestus in Greek myth, Wayland in Germanic tradition, and Svarog in Slavic lore. Smiths were often associated with creating the chief gods' weapons, such as Indra's Vajra or Zeus's lightning bolt. Smith figures were frequently depicted as lame, like Hephaestus and Wayland. They also shared motifs of crafting powerful items and even inventing mechanical wings, as seen in Daedalus and Wayland.

Role/Function: Underworld god
Notes: God of the underworld, often associated with cattle, magic, and oaths.

Role/Function: Cattle god, God of the night sky, rain, poetry, and fertility
Notes: Wélṇos is the younger brother of Dyēus Pətḗr and the king of the Ánsūs (chthonic deities). He is associated with the night sky and the chthonic aspects of fertility. His most well-known descendants are the Greek Uranus (Ouranos) and the Vedic Varuna. In mythology, Uranus is famously castrated by his son Cronus, which parallels some PIE mythological themes. Veles is a Slavic god of cattle, wealth, and the underworld.

Role/Function: Primordial twin
Notes: First mortal, often sacrificed to create the world.

Role/Function: Water god, associated with oceans and storms
Notes: Ápōm Népōts is the god of oceans and storms, the brother of Dyēus. In Vedic tradition, he is known as Apām Nápāt, a fire god associated with water.

Role/Function: Dawn goddess, light, youth, and spring
Notes: Áusōs, daughter of Dyeus, is the personification of dawn. Described as "reddish-mantled," "golden-armed," and "golden-haired." She rises from the ocean and heralds her sister Sāwḷ, the Sun. In some traditions, Áusōs is considered a mother of the Diwós Súnū.  Her children, the Diwós Súnū, are twin horse gods who pull the chariots of Sāwḷ (Sun) and Mḗnōts (Moon). Her mythological descendants include Eos (Greek), Aurora (Roman), and Ushas (Vedic).

Role/Function: Goddess of fertility, prosperity, and cows
Notes: Cóuwindā is one of the Children of Dā́nu, associated with the fertility and prosperity of the herd.

Role/Function: Goddess of water, fertility, and rivers
Greek: Tethys, Danaos, Danaoi, Danaids, Danae, Danaans
Ossetia: Donbettyr, Danube river, Don, Dnieper, Dniester
Notes: Mallory and Adams propose Deh₂nu as a lexical correspondence for river deities, but there’s limited evidence of a specific PIE river goddess. Seen as the mother of Deh₂newyóes. Linked to various river names and mythological tribes, including the Vedic Danavas and the Irish Tuatha Dé Danann. Associated with rivers like the Danube.

Role/Function: Earth mother, paired with Dyḗws
Notes: Personification of the earth. She is the consort of Dyḗws in many traditions and is depicted as a mother figure. Examples include Demeter in Greek mythology and Prithvi in Vedic myth.

Role/Function: Realm of the gods, the sky, ether, upper air, assembly of deities
Notes: Dyēus represents the realm of the gods and the upper sky, sometimes called Áḱmōn or Wḗis. It is the ether, or the air that gods breathe, which is poisonous to humans. Dyēus connects with the Milky Way (Swélcṃs) and is the home of the divine assembly where the gods sit on a golden seat (Sédlom Ausinā Deiwṓm). Beyond Dyēus lies Ghā́wos, the primordial void or chaos. Dyēus is begotten from the skull of Yemós and is closely associated with the divine and cosmic order.

Role/Function: Daylight sky personified as a male deity
Notes: The chief deity, associated with justice, honor, and the daylight sky. Often paired with the earth mother, Dʰéǵʰōm. Descendants include the Vedic Dyáuṣ and Greek Zeus.

Role/Function: Primordial earth goddess
Notes: Érā is the earth goddess in Proto-Indo-European mythos, representing fertility and abundance. She is the counterpart to Áḱmōn, the sky god.

Role/Function: Three primordial goddesses in charge of fate
Notes: Depicted as three goddesses controlling destiny and fate, often depicted as spinning the threads of life.

Role/Function: Underwater fire god
Notes: A god of water and underwater fire, often tied to fertility and purification. Appears in Vedic as Apam Napat and Roman Neptune.

Role/Function: Goddess of dawn, brings light
Notes: The dawn goddess, daughter of Dyḗws. Brings light to the world each day, reflected as Eos in Greek mythology and Ushas in Vedic tradition.

Role/Function: God of wind and storm
Notes: Represents the wind, a deity of air and storm. Seen in Vedic traditions as Vayu, and in Roman mythology as Venti.

Role/Function: Goddess of death, responsible for life and death
Notes: Goddess of death and the underworld, found in various traditions, including the Norse Hel and Slavic Morana.

Role/Function: God of fire and forge, creator of divine tools and weapons
Notes: Son of Dyēus Pətḗr and Diwónā, Ḷbhús is the god of fire, the forge, and craftsmanship. He is lame and considered unattractive, but he created the Golden Seat of the gods, Sāwḷ's chariot, and Perqū́nos's bronze axe and lightning bolts. Known to teach shaman-smiths how to smelt copper. Similar deities include Kothar-wa-Khasis (Egyptian) and Kagu-tsuchi (Japanese), though they are likely unrelated.

Role/Function: Cosmic law and order, contracts, and governance of waters
Notes: A pair of deities responsible for law, contracts, and order in the cosmos, akin to Mitra and Varuna in the Vedic tradition.

Role/Function: God of boundaries, associated with the planet Mercury and Wednesday
Notes: Pā́usōn, also known as Ōkúsōn, is associated with boundaries and the planet Mercury. He shares similarities with Hermes and Mercury, who are also gods of boundaries and associated with Wednesday.

Role/Function: Pastoral god, protector of roads and herds
Notes: God of roads, herds, and protector of travelers. Found in Vedic myth as Pūṣan and Roman myth as Faunus.

Role/Function: Thunder god, associated with lightning and war
Notes: The god of thunder and storms, often wielding a weapon like Zeus’ thunderbolt or Thor’s hammer. Appears in Vedic, Baltic, and Slavic mythologies.

Role/Function: Goddess of gardens, love, and fertility
Notes: Goddess of gardens and love, reflecting deities like Frigg in Norse mythology and Parvati in Vedic tradition.

Role/Function: God of seed, agriculture, and creation
Notes: Séwətōr represents the fertile principle of creation and agriculture, involved in the propagation of life.

Role/Function: God of cattle and livestock
Notes: God of cattle, wealth, and fertility, often linked to Veles in Slavic mythology and Velnias in Baltic traditions.
